facts.text: The Nintendo Game Boy sold over 118 million units worldwide, making it one of the most successful handhelds ever.
The original Game Boy launched in 1989 with a monochrome green LCD screen.
Tetris was the bundled launch title that helped drive Game Boy sales.
The Game Boy used four AA batteries and could last up to 30+ hours of play.
The Game Boy's durability and portability helped define handheld gaming.
The Game Boy Color sold over 50 million units worldwide.
The Game Boy Color added a color screen while maintaining compatibility with original Game Boy games.
The GBC improved performance and expanded memory compared to the original Game Boy.
Pokémon Gold and Silver were major system sellers for the Game Boy Color.
The Game Boy Color came in a wide variety of translucent shell colors.
The Game Boy Advance sold over 81 million units worldwide.
The GBA featured a 32-bit ARM processor, a major leap from the Game Boy Color.
The Game Boy Advance introduced shoulder buttons for more complex controls.
The GBA was backward compatible with original Game Boy and Game Boy Color games.
The GBA SP introduced a clamshell design with a front-lit screen.
The Nintendo DS sold over 154 million units worldwide, making it the best-selling handheld ever.
The DS introduced dual screens with touch input on the lower display.
The DS popularized stylus-based gameplay long before smartphones.
The Nintendo DS supported local wireless multiplayer without internet.
The DS was backward compatible with Game Boy Advance cartridges.
The Nintendo 3DS sold over 75 million units worldwide.
The 3DS featured glasses-free 3D visuals using parallax barrier technology.
The 3DS included an analog Circle Pad for improved control precision.
The Nintendo 3DS was backward compatible with Nintendo DS games.
The 3DS eShop enabled digital downloads and indie game distribution.
The PlayStation Portable (PSP) sold over 80 million units worldwide.
The PSP used optical UMD discs for game distribution.
The PSP supported multimedia features including music and video playback.
The PSP was one of the first handhelds to offer near-console-quality 3D graphics.
The PSP Go removed the UMD drive in favor of digital downloads only.
The PlayStation Vita sold around 15 million units worldwide.
The PS Vita featured an OLED touchscreen display on early models.
The Vita included rear touch controls for innovative gameplay input.
The PS Vita supported remote play with PlayStation 4 consoles.
The PS Vita struggled commercially but gained a strong cult following.
The Sega Game Gear sold around 10 million units worldwide.
The Game Gear featured a full-color backlit screen, rare for its time.
The Game Gear was essentially a portable version of the Sega Master System.
The Game Gear required six AA batteries for operation.
The Game Gear struggled against the Game Boy despite superior hardware.
The Neo Geo Pocket Color sold around 2 million units worldwide.
The Neo Geo Pocket Color featured excellent battery life and a clicky joystick.
The NGPC had a strong fighting game library from SNK.
The Neo Geo Pocket Color was fully backward compatible with monochrome NGPC games.
The NGPC competed briefly against the Game Boy Color.
The WonderSwan sold over 3 million units in Japan.
The WonderSwan was designed by Gunpei Yokoi, creator of the Game Boy.
The WonderSwan had both vertical and horizontal gameplay orientation modes.
The WonderSwan was only released in Japan but had strong local success.
The WonderSwan Color improved display and game library over the original model.
The Game Boy Micro was a compact redesign of the Game Boy Advance with a smaller form factor.
The Game Boy Micro featured interchangeable faceplates for customization.
The Game Boy Advance SP introduced a front-lit (later backlit) folding screen design.
The GBA SP was Nintendo’s first clamshell handheld design.
The Game Boy Advance line supported a massive library of both new and legacy games.
The Nintendo DS Lite improved brightness and battery life over the original DS.
The DS Lite was slimmer and more refined than the original Nintendo DS.
The Nintendo DSi added cameras and an online store for downloadable games.
The DSi removed the Game Boy Advance cartridge slot found in earlier models.
The DS family became one of the most diverse handheld lines in history.
The Nintendo 3DS XL featured a larger screen for improved visibility.
The New Nintendo 3DS added a second analog nub for better control.
The New 3DS improved processing power for enhanced game performance.
The 3DS line supported StreetPass for passive local multiplayer interactions.
The 3DS had a strong library of both first-party and third-party titles.
The PlayStation Portable Slim and Lite improved portability and battery life.
The PSP had a wide range of multimedia capabilities beyond gaming.
The PSP supported downloadable games through the PlayStation Store.
The PSP was popular for homebrew and emulation communities.
The PSP had a widescreen display uncommon in handhelds of its era.
The PlayStation Vita Slim reduced weight and improved battery efficiency.
The PS Vita supported remote play with PlayStation 4 consoles.
The Vita had one of the most powerful handheld GPUs of its time.
The PS Vita used proprietary memory cards instead of standard SD cards.
The PS Vita struggled commercially despite strong hardware and innovation.
The Game Gear had a full-color backlit screen, rare for early handhelds.
The Game Gear was essentially a portable Sega Master System.
The Game Gear required six AA batteries, limiting portability.
The Game Gear had a strong Sega arcade-style game library.
The Game Gear was more powerful than the Game Boy but less efficient.
The Neo Geo Pocket Color featured an extremely precise clicky joystick.
The NGPC had a reputation for excellent battery efficiency.
The Neo Geo Pocket Color had a strong fighting game lineup.
The NGPC was compatible with monochrome Neo Geo Pocket games.
The NGPC competed briefly with the Game Boy Color in select markets.
The WonderSwan was designed by Game Boy creator Gunpei Yokoi.
The WonderSwan could be played in both vertical and horizontal orientations.
The WonderSwan was only released in Japan but achieved strong sales there.
The WonderSwan Color improved screen quality and game library significantly.
The WonderSwan was known for its extremely long battery life.
The Atari Lynx was one of the first handhelds with a color LCD screen.
The Atari Lynx featured advanced hardware but struggled with battery life.
The Lynx supported multiplayer linking between multiple devices.
The Atari Lynx was more powerful than the original Game Boy.
The Lynx sold far fewer units than its competitors despite innovation.
The Nintendo Game & Watch series was one of the earliest portable gaming devices.
Game & Watch devices featured single-game LCD handheld experiences.
The Game & Watch introduced the D-pad, later used in the NES controller.
Game & Watch units were extremely popular in the early 1980s.
The Game & Watch line laid the foundation for Nintendo’s handheld dominance.
